cross-sectional.name: Cross-sectional study
cross-sectional.description: Measures exposure and outcome at a single point in time to estimate prevalence and associations.
cross-sectional.when_to_use: Best for prevalence estimation, hypothesis generation, and snapshot comparisons across groups.
strengths: Can evaluate multiple exposures and outcomes
limitations: Selection and survival bias may distort estimates

cohort.name: Cohort study
cohort.description: Follows exposed and unexposed groups over time to estimate incidence and risk differences.
cohort.when_to_use: Best when exposure precedes outcome and you want incidence, risk, or prognosis estimates.
strengths: Can estimate incidence and relative risk
Supports multiple outcomes from one exposure
limitations: Loss to follow-up may bias results

case-control.name: Case-control study
case-control.description: Compares prior exposures between people with an outcome (cases) and without (controls).
case-control.when_to_use: Best for rare outcomes or long-latency diseases when prospective follow-up is impractical.
strengths: Faster and cheaper than many cohort studies
Can assess multiple exposures for one outcome
limitations: Recall and selection bias are common

ecological.name: Ecological study
ecological.description: Analyzes associations using group-level data (e.g., regions, schools, countries) rather than individual-level data.
ecological.when_to_use: Best for policy comparisons, broad population trends, and hypotheses using aggregate data.
strengths: Useful when only aggregate data are available
Good for broad policy and geographic comparisons
Can be fast using existing datasets

RCT.name: Randomized controlled trial
RCT.description: Randomly allocates interventions to estimate causal effects with reduced confounding.
RCT.when_to_use: Best for intervention effectiveness or efficacy when randomization is feasible and ethical.
strengths: Strong internal validity for causal inference
Balances measured and unmeasured confounders on average

natural experiment.name: Natural experiment
natural experiment.description: Uses external or policy-driven variation in exposure assignment to infer causal effects.
natural experiment.when_to_use: Best when randomization is impossible but a plausibly exogenous event or policy change exists.
strengths: Can estimate causal effects in real-world settings
Often improves external validity versus tightly controlled trials
limitations: Identification assumptions can be hard to verify
Design quality depends on the natural assignment process
